title: Chapter Ten: The Breaking Point
content: He carried it in like an offering.

The canvas was large — nearly four feet tall — draped in black velvet, and Julian held it with both hands, his knuckles white against the fabric, his face bright with something that looked like joy but sat wrong on his bones. He'd been working on it for weeks. I'd heard him through the walls at night, the scratch and whisper of bristles, sometimes a muttered curse, sometimes silence so complete I forgot he was there at all. But I hadn't seen it. He'd kept it covered, turned to the wall, hidden like a confession he wasn't ready to make.
Now he set it on the easel by the window and pulled the velvet away.
The light from the west-facing window caught it like a prayer answered.
It was stunning.

I don't use that word lightly. I've grown up in a house full of paintings — bad ones, haunted ones, ones that watch you from corners with eyes that follow — and I know what oil on canvas can do when the hands are right. Julian's hands were right. The portrait showed me standing in the garden, the black eyed Susans in bloom around my ankles, the Vale spreading behind me in layers of green and fog. The light was golden, honeyed, the kind of light that only exists in memory or forgery. The brushwork on the dress was extraordinary — you could almost feel the linen, the way it caught the wind and pressed against my waist. The garden was so real I could smell the loam, the green growing things, the wet stones of the path.
But the face.
The face was wrong.

It was my face — the shape of it, the cheekbones, the dark brows. But he'd made me softer. Rounder in the jaw, gentler in the mouth, the eyes wider and more trusting. He'd taken the angles out of me, the wariness, the set of my jaw that I've earned through years of living in a house that eats women. He'd made me pliable. He'd painted me the way the curse wanted me — open, yielding, ready.
"Marry me," he said.
The words landed in the room like stones dropped into still water. Ripples moved outward from them, and I felt each one in my chest.

"We're meant to be, Aurelia." He stepped closer. The light from the window caught his hair, gilded the edges of him until he looked like something from a tapestry — the prince, the hero, the answer to every question the Vale had ever asked. "I've known it since I arrived. Since the first time I saw you in this house, I knew."

He was selling destiny. I could hear it in the cadence, the rhythm of a man who had rehearsed this, who believed in the performance. And the worst part — the part that turned my blood to something sluggish and foreign — was that my body bought it.

I felt myself lean toward him. Not a choice. My spine curved, my shoulders tilted, my body a compass needle finding its north. My lips parted. The word *yes* formed in my throat like a pearl, layer by luminous layer, and I could not stop it rising.
The curse was not metaphor. It was physical.

My hand lifted — my left hand, the one nearest to him — and extended, fingers opening, as if waiting for a ring that wasn't coming. Because he hadn't brought a ring. He'd brought a painting. A painting of me the way he wanted me to be. And still my hand reached for him like a marionette's, the strings pulled by something ancient and patient that lived in the stones of this house.

I wanted to say no. I *wanted* to. The wanting was real, and it was mine, and it burned in me like a coal that wouldn't go out. But the wanting and the doing had become two separate countries, and there was no bridge between them, and my mouth was opening and the word *yes* was rising through me like bile and I could not —
I could not —

My hands moved without my consent. They reached for him. My fingers brushed the lapel of his coat and the fabric was warm from his body and I could smell him — turpentine and cedar and something underneath that was just *man*, just the animal reality of him, and the curse sang in my blood YES YES YES, a chorus of dead women whose bodies had learned the steps before their minds could object.
My mouth opened.
The word was at my teeth.
The scream cleaved the room.

I had never — in my entire life — heard Nadine raise her voice. Not once. Not during my parents' funeral, not during the long winters when the pipes froze and the roof leaked and we sat in coats in the parlor, not when I fell from the apple tree at nine and split my chin open on a rock. She spoke in whispers. She moved through the house like a woman trying not to wake the dead, which, in Blackwood Vale, was a reasonable concern.

But this was not a whisper. This was a sound that came from somewhere below her ribs, from a place so deep and so long buried that the unearthings of it tore something open on the way out. It wasn't just volume. It was *force*. The scream moved through the air like a blade — I felt it pass through me, a cold hot shock that split the air itself, and for one crystalline second the world went sharp and clean and clear.
The spell broke.
One second. That was all. But one second was enough.
In that second, I saw Julian.

Not the Julian I'd been seeing for weeks — the charming smile, the warm eyes, the careful attentiveness that had made me doubt my own wariness. The charm flickered like a dying bulb, and what it revealed underneath made my stomach drop. His smile was a rictus — hungry, stretched, the teeth too visible. His eyes went cold, not angry but *calculating*, the way a chess player looks at a board when a piece moves unexpectedly. For one terrible instant I saw the thing behind the mask: something old, something patient, something that wanted me the way the house wanted me — not for love, not for companionship, but for consumption.
Then the mask snapped back. Smooth as water closing over a stone.

"Take your time," he said softly. Reasonably. The warmth back in his voice, the gentle concern. But the room had dropped ten degrees, and I could feel the cold on my skin like an accusation, like the house itself had flinched away from what he was.

My hand was still on his lapel. I pulled it back. The motion was mine — I felt the difference, the way my muscles obeyed my thoughts instead of the curse's. The one second was fading, the threads reknitting, but I'd used it. I'd seen him. And I'd gotten my hand back.
"No," I said.

Quietly. Firmly. The word tasted like copper and freedom — the metallic bite of blood in the mouth, the sharp clean air after a storm. I held the taste of it on my tongue, let it sit there, let it prove to me that I had spoken it.

Julian's face did something complicated. Surprise first — genuine, I think, or as genuine as he could manage. Then assessment, quick and cold behind the eyes, a recalculation. Then cold itself, just for a moment, a flash of winter that made the room's temperature drop feel like a fire by comparison. He looked at me the way you'd look at a lock that wouldn't turn.
Then the mask settled again. Seamless.
"Of course," he said. "I understand."

He didn't understand. He wasn't accepting my answer. He was shelving it. I could see it in the way he straightened his cuffs, the way his smile didn't waver — he'd computed the variable and adjusted. He would try again. The curse was patient, and so was he. They had all the time in the Vale, and the Vale kept its women a long, long time.
He covered the portrait with the velvet again. The gesture was almost tender. Then he left, and the door closed behind him with the soft click of a cell locking.
Nadine was sitting up straight.

I had never seen her sit up straight. In my whole life, she had been a curved shape in the corner chair — spine bent, shoulders curved inward, her body a question mark, a woman folded into herself like paper crammed into an envelope. But now she was rigid in her chair, both hands gripping the armrests so hard the tendons stood out like cords, her knuckles bone-white, her chest heaving.
She was breathing like she'd run miles.
"You said no," she whispered.

Her voice cracked on the second word. She said it the way you say things you can't believe — a prayer, a diagnosis, a number that comes up on a test result that changes everything. Like it was a miracle. Like I'd walked on water or raised the dead or done something that shouldn't have been possible.
"I heard your voice," I said. My own voice was strange to me — hoarse, raw, like I'd been the one screaming. "You broke the spell."

Nadine's hands began to shake. Not a small tremor — a full vibration that moved from her fingers up through her wrists and into her forearms, her whole body a tuning fork struck against something terrible. She released the armrests and pressed her palms flat against her thighs, trying to still them. It didn't work.
"For a second," she said. "I could only break it for a second."

I knelt beside her chair. Up close, I could see the cost of what she'd done — her eyes were bloodshot, the veins in her temples visible, her skin gray-white and damp. She'd torn something open inside herself to make that sound. She'd pulled the scream up from wherever she'd buried it, from decades of silence, from the place where the curse had sewn her mouth shut and taught her that quiet was survival.
Her hands kept shaking.
"I couldn't break mine," she said. So quiet I almost didn't hear it. "Not when it was my turn."
The words hung in the air between us. I understood them, and understanding them was like swallowing glass.

She meant her own proposal. Her own Julian — not the same man, but the same *thing*, wearing a different face, the curse cycling through its suits of skin. She'd had her moment, her painting, her compulsion. And when it happened, she hadn't screamed. She hadn't been able to. The curse had been too deep in her by then, too thorough, and her voice had stayed locked in the vault of her chest where it had been since she was young enough to still hope.
But today — for me — she'd found it.
She'd found the scream she couldn't find for herself.
"Nadine," I said.

She shook her head. She didn't want gratitude. She didn't want acknowledgment. What she wanted, I think, was to sit with the fact that she had done one thing right in forty years of wrong silence, and that the one thing had cost her something she couldn't name.
I sat on the floor beside her chair and said nothing more.

That night, we sat together in the parlor. No fire, no candles — just the thin moonlight through the windows, casting the room in shades of gray and black. Nadine was in her usual chair, but she sat differently, closer to the edge, as if preparing to rise. Or to scream again.

We didn't speak. There was nothing to say that the silence hadn't already said better. But the silence was different now — not the silence of two strangers sharing a cursed house, the careful not-touching, the orbits that never intersect. This was the silence of two people who had been in the same war, different fronts. The silence of survivors.
I wrote in my journal by moonlight, the pencil moving slowly across the page:
*My grandmother screamed and the world broke open for one second. One second was enough.*
*She couldn't scream for herself. But she screamed for me.*
*That's the difference between a curse and a choice. A curse makes you silent. A choice makes you loud.*

I closed the journal. In her chair, Nadine's breathing had evened out — not asleep, but resting, the way you rest after a long illness breaks. The shaking had stopped. Her hands lay open in her lap, palm up, empty.

Outside, the Vale was dark and the house breathed around us, patient as a curse, and somewhere Julian was turning the problem of me over in his mind, recomputing, recalibrating, preparing to try again.

One second had been enough. But the curse had more than a second. The curse had years. It had generations. It had the stones of this house and the soil of this valley and every Buena woman who had ever said yes when she meant no.
I sat with my grandmother in the dark and listened to her breathe, and I did not fool myself that we were safe.
We were not safe.
We were loud. And for now, that was enough.
